competency_cluster: cluster_02: Recognizing and Representing Proportional Relationships
coconstruction_stages: Show students a simple ratio table and ask them to predict what makes a relationship proportional.; Guide students to test whether ratios are equivalent by calculating unit rates or cross-multiplying.; Have students examine a graph and identify what they notice about points that show proportional versus non-proportional relationships.; Ask students to articulate the difference between testing ratios and analyzing graphs as two valid methods.; Collaboratively build the rubric levels by sorting student work samples from weakest to strongest.
coconstruction_student_prompts: What do you notice about the ratios when a relationship is proportional?; How can you check if two ratios are equivalent without a calculator?; What does a proportional relationship look like on a graph compared to one that is not proportional?; When you test ratios or look at a graph, what tells you that a relationship is NOT proportional?
coconstruction_anchor_examples_guidance: Select work samples that show clear differences: one where a student tests ratios correctly and concludes proportionality accurately, one where a student attempts to test ratios but makes a calculation error, and one where a student correctly analyzes a graph. Avoid examples with multiple errors or ambiguous reasoning.
student_rubric_no_evidence: I have not yet attempted to test ratios or analyze a graph to determine if a relationship is proportional.
student_rubric_emerging: I can test some ratios or describe features of a graph with help, but I do not always reach the correct conclusion about whether a relationship is proportional.
student_rubric_developing: I can independently test equivalent ratios or analyze a graph, but I am not consistent in determining whether a relationship is proportional.
student_rubric_competent: I can independently identify whether two quantities are in a proportional relationship by accurately testing equivalent ratios or analyzing a graph.
student_rubric_extending: I can identify proportional relationships and explain why a relationship fails the proportionality test.
student_rubric_self_checks: Did I test the ratios by finding unit rates or cross-multiplying, or did I analyze the graph to check if it passes through the origin and is a straight line?; Is my conclusion about proportionality correct based on my method, and can I explain why the relationship is or is not proportional?
feedback_moves_no_evidence: Ask the student to pick one ratio pair from the table and show you how to check if they are equivalent.; Point to a graph and ask: does this line go through the origin, and does every point fit the same pattern?
feedback_moves_emerging: Review the calculation step-by-step and ask the student to check their arithmetic or unit rate.; Ask: if this ratio works, does the next ratio also work the same way, or is something different?
feedback_moves_developing: Have the student test one more ratio pair or analyze a different part of the graph to build consistency.; Ask the student to state their conclusion in a complete sentence and then verify it matches their test results.
feedback_moves_competent: Ask the student to explain what would happen to the graph or ratios if the relationship were NOT proportional.; Invite the student to test a relationship that is close to proportional but not quite, and explain the difference.

competency_cluster: cluster_02: Recognizing and Representing Proportional Relationships
coconstruction_stages: Show students a simple proportional relationship and ask them to name all the different ways they could show it.; Guide students to sort their ideas into categories: numbers in a table, a picture or graph, a rule or equation, and other ways.; Work together to describe what makes each representation correct or incomplete, building criteria for each form.; Agree on what it means to show the same relationship in multiple ways and what mistakes might happen.
coconstruction_student_prompts: If I tell you that every 2 apples costs 3 dollars, how many different ways can you write or draw this?; Which of these representations shows the same relationship, and which ones have mistakes?; What would someone need to see in a table, graph, and equation to know they are all showing the same proportional relationship?; When you move from a table to a graph to an equation, what stays the same and what changes?
coconstruction_anchor_examples_guidance: Choose examples that show a proportional relationship represented correctly in at least two forms and one example with an error in one representation so students can identify what breaks the connection between forms. Include a simple ratio like 2:3 or 1:4 that students can verify across all representations.
student_rubric_no_evidence: I have not yet tried to show a proportional relationship in any form.
student_rubric_emerging: I can show a proportional relationship in one form with help, but my work has mistakes or missing parts.
student_rubric_developing: I can show a proportional relationship in two different forms on my own, but the forms do not match perfectly.
student_rubric_competent: I can accurately show a proportional relationship using tables, graphs, equations, and other forms that all match.
student_rubric_extending: I can move smoothly between different representations and explain why they show the same proportional relationship.
student_rubric_self_checks: Can I show this relationship in at least two different ways, and do they tell the same story?; If I check one point from my table in my graph and equation, do all three agree?
feedback_moves_no_evidence: Ask the student to pick one form they feel most comfortable with and start there.; Provide a partially completed table or graph and ask the student to finish it and describe what they see.
feedback_moves_emerging: Have the student check one point from their representation against the original relationship to find where the error is.; Guide them to create a second representation by asking what the same relationship would look like in a different form.
feedback_moves_developing: Ask the student to pick a point and trace it through both representations to find where they do not match.; Encourage them to create a third representation and check if all three forms agree on the same points.
feedback_moves_competent: Ask the student to explain how they know their table, graph, and equation are all showing the same relationship.; Challenge them to create a fourth representation or to predict what would happen if the constant of proportionality changed.

competency_cluster: cluster_03: Identifying and Using the Constant of Proportionality
coconstruction_stages: Show students a simple proportional relationship in one representation and ask them what stays the same no matter how the numbers change.; Guide students to discover the constant by comparing pairs of values across two or three different representations of the same relationship.; Have students name what they found and decide what it means to identify the constant correctly versus incorrectly.; Work together to describe what identifying the constant looks like across all five forms: tables, graphs, equations, diagrams, and words.; Sort anchor examples by level and discuss why some show the constant clearly while others show confusion or missing work.
coconstruction_student_prompts: What number or ratio stays the same when we look at different points in this relationship?; How can you find that same constant in a table, a graph, and an equation?; What would it look like if someone found the constant in only one representation but not in others?; How would you explain to a friend what the constant of proportionality is and why it matters?
coconstruction_anchor_examples_guidance: Choose examples that show the constant of proportionality in at least two different representations so students can see consistency, and include one example where a student confuses the constant with another number in the problem. Select a mix where the constant is a whole number and a fraction to show variety.
student_rubric_no_evidence: I have not yet attempted to identify the constant of proportionality.
student_rubric_emerging: I can identify the constant of proportionality in one representation with help, but I make mistakes or am not sure when I try on my own.
student_rubric_developing: I can identify the constant of proportionality on my own in tables and graphs, but I struggle when it is shown as an equation, diagram, or words.
student_rubric_competent: I can identify the constant of proportionality correctly in tables, graphs, equations, diagrams, and verbal descriptions.
student_rubric_extending: I can identify the constant of proportionality and explain why it stays the same across all different representations of the same relationship.
student_rubric_self_checks: Can I find the constant in at least three different forms: a table, a graph, and an equation or words?; Does the constant I found make sense when I check it with another pair of values in the relationship?
feedback_moves_no_evidence: Ask the student to pick any two related values from a table and find the ratio between them.; Model finding the constant in one representation aloud, then ask the student to try the same process with a different table.
feedback_moves_emerging: Have the student identify the constant in a familiar representation, then guide them to find the same constant in a second representation by asking what should stay the same.; Ask the student to check their constant by testing it with a different pair of values to build confidence and reduce errors.
feedback_moves_developing: Provide the constant and ask the student to verify it appears in an unfamiliar representation like an equation or diagram.; Scaffold the student through one example of finding the constant in a new form, then release responsibility for the next example.
feedback_moves_competent: Ask the student to identify the constant in two representations and explain why both show the same value.; Challenge the student to predict what the constant would be in a new representation before finding it, then verify.

competency_cluster: cluster_04: Solving Real-World Proportional Relationship Problems
lt_name: Applying Proportional Relationships to Real-World Contexts
coconstruction_stages: Show students a real-world scenario involving proportions and ask them to identify what stays the same and what changes.; Guide students to distinguish between incomplete attempts and accurate applications by examining sample work together.; Have students articulate what makes a solution multistep and what reasoning must be shown.; Discuss how applying proportions to unfamiliar or complex situations differs from routine problems.
coconstruction_student_prompts: What quantities in this problem are connected proportionally, and how do you know?; What would it look like if someone tried to use a proportion but made a mistake or stopped too early?; What steps do you need to take to fully solve a problem with proportions, and why is each step necessary?; How would you explain your choice of strategy if the problem were more complex or used a context you'd never seen before?
coconstruction_anchor_examples_guidance: Select anchor examples that show the progression from a single incomplete proportion application to a multistep solution with clear reasoning, and include one example where a student adapts their strategy to an unfamiliar context. Ensure examples reflect realistic student work rather than polished solutions.
student_rubric_no_evidence: I have not yet attempted to use proportional relationships to solve a problem.
student_rubric_emerging: I can identify a proportional relationship with help, but I apply it incompletely or make errors in my solution.
student_rubric_developing: I can apply proportional relationships to solve a problem on my own, but I may skip a step or leave out some of my reasoning.
student_rubric_competent: I can independently apply proportional relationships to solve multistep real-world problems and show all my reasoning clearly.
student_rubric_extending: I can apply proportional relationships to new or complex situations and explain why I chose my solution strategy.
student_rubric_self_checks: Did I identify which quantities are proportional and set up the relationship correctly?; Did I complete all the steps needed to solve the problem and explain my reasoning at each step?
feedback_moves_no_evidence: Ask the student to point out two quantities in the problem that might be related and describe how they change together.; Model identifying a proportional relationship aloud, then ask the student to try the same thinking on a similar problem.
feedback_moves_emerging: Have the student check their proportion setup by testing it with a simple value and asking if the relationship makes sense.; Guide the student to complete one more step by asking what information they still need to find to answer the full question.
feedback_moves_developing: Ask the student to trace through their work and identify which step answers which part of the question.; Prompt the student to add a sentence explaining why they chose their strategy or how they knew their answer was reasonable.
feedback_moves_competent: Present a proportional problem with an unfamiliar context and ask the student to explain how their strategy would change.; Challenge the student to solve the same problem using a different method and compare the efficiency of both approaches.
